meta.title: Mercedes SLS AMG R197 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

@graph.name: Mercedes SLS AMG R197 Engine Codes
Mercedes SLS AMG R197 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M159 engine for the SLS AMG Roadster typically costs £8,000–£20,000 supply only, depending on specification — the standard 571 bhp unit sits at £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500 due to the front-mid-engine layout. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three M159 variants (standard, GT, Black Series) share the same fundamental engine architecture — a hand-built 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 with dry-sump lubrication. The standard 571 bhp unit is slightly less stressed than the higher-output versions and is therefore considered marginally more reliable. However, all M159 engines are robust when properly maintained.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes SLS AMG R197?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M159 V8 can suffer from valve bucket wear ("tick") on cold start (20k–40k miles). The dry-sump oil system is prone to leaks at the oil tank connections and cooler lines. Timing chain and camshaft adjuster wear can occur on higher-mileage examples. The AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7-speed transmission can suffer from clutch wear and mechatronic issues. The engine is complex and requires specialist AMG knowledge for maintenance and repair.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes SLS AMG R197 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The SLS AMG is considered one of the most robust AMG models. The M159 engine is a heavily developed version of the M156, with improved valve train components and dry-sump lubrication addressing many of the earlier engine's weaknesses. With proper maintenance — including regular valve clearance checks, oil changes and transmission servicing — the M159 is capable of high mileages. However, maintenance costs are substantial due to the engine's complexity and AMG-specific parts.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Black Series (631 bhp) is the most extreme and highest-strung M159 variant, with a higher redline and more aggressive cam profile. While it's a phenomenal engine, its rarity and high-strung nature mean it requires even more meticulous maintenance and carries higher replacement costs. The standard 571 bhp unit is the most sensible choice for most buyers.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my SLS AMG R197 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Persistent cold-start tapping that doesn't subside (valve bucket wear), visible oil leaks from the dry-sump system, rattling noises on cold start (camshaft adjuster wear), blue smoke (valve seals), or a sudden loss of power are key warnings. Any unusual noises or warning lights warrant immediate inspection by an AMG specialist.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: With strong residual values — a well-kept R197 Roadster can be worth £60,000–£150,000 — engine replacement is almost always financially viable. An £8,000–£14,000 recon sits at 5–23% of the car's value. Even the Black Series' £12,000–£20,000 recon sits at 8–33% of a top-end example's value. Replacement is justifiable if the car is otherwise in excellent condition and retains its collector value.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M159 is capable of exceeding 100,000 miles with proper maintenance, though many SLS AMGs are low-mileage collector cars. Valve bucket wear typically appears around 20k–40k miles; timing chain and cam adjuster wear around 30k–60k miles. Regular maintenance — valve clearance checks, oil changes, transmission servicing — is essential to achieve high mileages.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the R197 Roadster and the C197 Coupé?

acceptedAnswer.text: The R197 is the Roadster (convertible) version of the SLS AMG, featuring a fabric soft-top that can be operated at speeds up to 50 km/h. The C197 is the Coupé with the iconic gullwing doors. Both share the same M159 engine, drivetrain and platform. The Roadster is slightly heavier due to chassis reinforcement for the open-top design, and it features different rear-end styling. Engine costs are identical; body-specific parts differ. The C197 Coupé is covered on a separate generation page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes SLS AMG R197 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen, lower passenger side), stamped on the engine block, and detailed in your vehicle registration documents (V5C). The M159 engine code is found on the left side of the engine block near the front, requiring removal of the engine cover. Tell us your variant (SLS AMG, SLS AMG GT, or Black Series) and we'll confirm it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will verify it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes SLS AMG R197 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the SLS AMG Roadster — the hand-built M159 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 in 571 bhp and 591 bhp GT forms, plus the 631 bhp Black Series. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data on AMG's gullwing icon. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes SLS AMG R197 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the SLS AMG Roadster specifically — the M159 6.2-litre V8 in standard, GT and Black Series forms.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG Roadster R197 — the open-top version of the gullwing coupé — was introduced in 2011 as a 2012 model year car. While the C197 Coupé celebrated the legendary 300 SL with its gullwing doors, the R197 Roadster offered a fabric soft-top that could be operated at speeds up to 50 km/h, folding away in 11 seconds. Power came exclusively from the hand-built M159 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 — a heavily developed version of the M156, featuring dry-sump lubrication, forged internals and over 120 unique components. The Roadster was offered in standard 571 bhp form, the uprated 591 bhp GT specification from 2012, and the extreme 631 bhp Black Series from 2013. The SLS AMG was the first Mercedes-Benz designed from scratch entirely by AMG. Production of the R197 Roadster ended in 2014.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes SLS (aluminium spaceframe) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz SLR McLaren • Successor: Mercedes-AMG GT (C190) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C197 Coupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No [MB-VERIFIED] enquiry data exists for the SLS AMG R197 — the model is absent from EM_Proprietary_Data's per-model table, reflecting its low-volume, ultra-premium UK status.

items.quote: The SLS AMG Roadster's engine range is defined by one legendary powerplant — the M159 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 — offered in three states of tune. The standard 571 bhp M159 (SLS AMG Roadster) is the purest expression of AMG's naturally aspirated V8 era: 6208 cc, 420 kW, 650 Nm of torque, and a spine-tingling 7200 rpm redline. It's a hand-built masterpiece with dry-sump lubrication and forged internals. The 591 bhp GT variant offers an additional 20 bhp and a slightly sharper chassis setup, with the same fundamental reliability as the base engine. The 631 bhp Black Series is the ultimate evolution — higher redline (8000 rpm), revised valve train and intake routing — but it's rare and its extreme nature means higher running costs. The M159 is generally considered more robust than its M156 predecessor, with improved valve buckets and revised camshafts addressing many of the earlier engine's weaknesses. However, the dry-sump system requires specialist maintenance, and the engine's complexity means repairs are expensive. Buying instruction: all three variants share the same fundamental M159 architecture — choose based on budget and desired specification. The standard 571 bhp car offers the best value; the GT adds a worthwhile power bump; the Black Series is for collectors and track-day enthusiasts.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Mercedes SLS AMG R197 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Legendary V8 — robust but costly to maintain
More power — same reliability as base

economicsRule.title: The R197 rule
economicsRule.text: A £8,000–£14,000 M159 recon against a £60,000–£150,000 R197 sits at 5–23% of the car's value — well within the 70% Mercedes threshold for almost every example. Even the Black Series' £12,000–£20,000 recon sits at 8–33% of a well-kept example's value. The SLS's strong residual values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable — the question is whether the car's overall condition and mileage justify the spend.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R197 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Standard 571 bhp M159 — the most common variant, best parts availability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R197 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Black Series (631 bhp) — rare, higher-strung, specialist-only maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R197 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Standard 571 bhp M159 — the purest expression of the M159 with the lowest running costs.

problems.title: M159 Valve Bucket Wear ("Tick")
problems.rootCause: The M159's valve buckets can suffer from "bleed down," resulting in excessive valve lash during cold starts, heard as a loud tapping noise until the engine reaches operating temperature. While the M159 features an improved valve bucket design over the M156, the issue can still occur. Camshaft wear is also a known issue on high-mileage examples.
problems.recommendation: On any SLS AMG approaching 20,000 miles, have the valve clearance checked — a £500–£1,500 preventative inspection is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored. Listen for cold-start tapping; if it persists beyond a few seconds, get it checked.

cta.label: Compare M159 engine prices for your SLS AMG →

problems.title: M159 Dry-Sump System Oil Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M159's dry-sump lubrication system uses an external oil tank and multiple oil lines. The system can develop leaks at the oil tank connections, cooler lines or the sump pan seals. Crankshaft sealing ring leaks and porosity in the timing case cover are also documented issues.
problems.recommendation: On any SLS AMG, check for oil leaks at every service — a £500–£1,500 seal replacement is cheaper than the £1,800–£3,500 repair that follows if ignored. The dry-sump system is a complex but essential part of the M159's design.

cta.label: Compare M159 engine prices for your SLS AMG →

problems.title: M159 Timing Chain and Camshaft Adjuster Wear
problems.affectedModels: All R197 SLS AMG variants (M159.980), particularly higher-mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: The M159 uses a duplex roller chain for camshaft drive. While the chain itself is robust, the camshaft adjusters can wear over time, leading to rattling noises on cold start and, in severe cases, timing issues. Camshaft journals have also been reported to wear on high-mileage M156/M159 engines.
problems.recommendation: If you hear rattling on cold start beyond the initial oil pressure build-up, get the timing system inspected — a £1,500–£3,000 preventative repair is cheaper than the £3,000–£6,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M159 engine prices for your SLS AMG →

problems.title: AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7-Speed Transmission Issues
problems.rootCause: The Getrag 7DCL750 7-speed dual-clutch transmission is a complex unit that can suffer from clutch wear, mechatronic unit failures and software issues. Hard shifts, delayed engagement and transmission warning lights are common symptoms.
problems.recommendation: On any SLS AMG, change the transmission fluid every 20,000 miles — a £500–£1,200 service is cheaper than the £3,500–£6,500 replacement that follows if ignored. The DCT is a critical component; neglect is expensive.

cta.label: Compare SLS AMG transmission repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: R197 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are R197 (SLS AMG Roadster, 2011–2014) only. The C197 Coupé shares the same M159 engine but is covered on a separate Generation Page per the one-page-per-body-code rule. The GT variant (591 bhp) was introduced in 2012; the Black Series (631 bhp) was introduced in 2013.
